title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: This is true for ALL majors at a university. The purpose of undergraduate studies is to prepare you for graduate studies, not the job market. My recommendation is that most students should just major in math at a university, learn the theories. Then learn job skills like programming, databases, web development, cloud computing, dev ops, and even machine learning on your own.
author: @Yavin4

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Here's the thing.  You should study what you love, and if it pays the bills, that's even better.  If you love computer programming, then computer science is the major for you.  If you hate computer programming but only in it for the money, then you will suffer and won't enjoy it.  The truth is most people do not like computer programming.

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Computer Science as a major is not there to get a job in Software Engineering, it was you who was mistaken. It's not that the major is useless. If you want to become a researcher in compilers or graphics theory, of course, the classes are a huge advantage. Telling people not to get a bachelor's is very irresponsible of you. Especially as an educated person from Georgia Tech, btw, I'm also a Georgia Tech grad. CS gives you a platform of knowledge; it's not a way to learn how to do Web Apps, you can do that in 3 months.

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Great advice, really.  I have worked as a software engineer, mostly in the embedded field, for the last 15 years.  My original major was in industrial engineering and I picked up a second degree in electrical engineering.  I had to learn the kinds of problems I wanted to help solve in order to find a lasting career, and developing software solutions has always seemed like the best bet.  However, even in the 1990s and the 2000s, I never considered taking computer science; because, as you mentioned, it delved into a lot of theory that did not seem useful in the real world - for what I seem to lean toward.  This can happened with any major, though, and much of the perception of "useful" information (or not) depends on one's own goals.  I have always loved the interaction between digital circuits, software, and real world systems, so now I work as a controls software engineer.......  On the other hand, AI is very interesting to me, where it applies to robotics and real world systems, so I am thinking about learning more about AI through free online courses, or wherever else.  A lifetime of continuous learning is the ticket, you know.
author: @chrisanderson5046

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: not sure where this gentleman went to school but a computer scientist is a software engineer and a software architect. yes things are a little bit slow now for software engineers but it's going to pick up. we don't have enough Software engineers. take my word for it. software engineers and computer scientists are the most powerful and important people on the planet. check out the super technology. only you can build it.
author: @morpheus9589

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: I avoided studying computer science at uni and instead went for business information systems/computing & IT cos I just wanted to learn software engineering and avoid all the maths stuff related to computer science. Later on  I actually got into maths and computer science (still learning) cos I wanted to understand more about computer architecture and overall computer chip design plus understanding how 2D/3D graphics work in video games , learning web dev/app dev as part of being a software engineer is cool but there are many other software engineering jobs  or even electronics engineering jobs which require you to apply complex maths algorithms and other stuff  you might learn in a CS degree or course. A lot of the youtubers talk about getting a job at google or Meta as a back end engineer or whatever  never about getting a job at meta or google working on their computer chips for AI though , the last job will require all that complex maths algorithms that you probably won't need if you just want to be a web developer  and in many cases you'd still need to know how to code because you might need to understand hardware description languages

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: The problem I see with young people going into computer science is they don't fully understand what it entails or how challenging it really is. They go into it with dollar signs in their eyes (usually put there by their parents) and a limited math background. I've seen many of these students drop out after 1-2 semesters, usually changing their major to IT or IS.

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Im currently studying electrical engeneering with a focus on information technology on a."Hochschule" - you get a bachelors but you have at least two laboratories per semester - and from the beginning on you get hands on experience!! Now im in 3-4 semester and i learned how to effectively program microconctrollers with asm or c, how to build ethernet networks with c++, how to create digital circuits with VHDL, this semester i will also learn about modelling and simulation in Python. Obviously thats alk besides all the hardware, physics and maths that we had to write exams on but these are just the tools to be able to do things! I can only invite you guys: try electrical engineering, you are 100% going to write code and its gonna be fun :)

title: Georgia Tech students feel strongly about the easiest engineering major on campus #bridgemoney
body:
Industrial Engineering is easier but it’s still very good tho, I think it translates better into higher level management.. like director, VP, and officer roles since a lot of times those are the ones who have to do with improving processes and making departments more efficient.. Industrial Engineering focuses particularly on that.. not actually making the products but making the production process of those products more streamlined and efficient..

My coworker who is an IE (he’s a VP of operations at his current job) makes a lot of bread and he described it with an analogy to lego toys..

Most Engineers are designing the individual “lego blocks” (components) that allow the vendors to build their toys (their solution/application)..
meanwhile the Industrial engineer is designing the “factory” that produces those individual Lego Blocks..

Say a factory staffed with engineers creates 10,000 lego blocks in a month, the industrial engineer is going to make it so instead of 10,000 they create 20,000 and for a cheaper cost per block, without having to hire new engineers..

They don’t create or improve the lego blocks themselves, they create or improve the system that produces those blocks..

If you wonder where the systems engineer fits into this analogy (since sometimes those get confused).. well they’re the ones making the “manual” to build the entire Lego set using those blocks, making sure that each individual Lego block fits and is compatible with the other components in reference to the completed lego set..

Hope this helps..
author: @GabrielGarcia-u8v

title: Georgia Tech students feel strongly about the easiest engineering major on campus #bridgemoney

body: Magical, Intense, Business, No BS, No Yelling At Each Other, No Flipping Tables, No Babyfaces, No Heels. Just two guys who want to be #1 and who have earned that spot! This is Professional Wrestling! This is what I got me to be a wrestling fan 39 years ago. Lets Do This!
